content:
# Genesis Chapter 47 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 27v–28r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_047_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_047_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE | **LXX Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/GEN_047_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/GEN_047_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`
**View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0051 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0052

---

*Joseph Presents His Brothers and Father to Pharaoh — Goshen Granted (vv. 1–6)*

**47:1** And Joseph came [וַיָּבֹא יוֹסֵף] and told Pharaoh [וַיַּגֵּד לְפַרְעֹה] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: My father [אָבִי] and my brothers [וְאַחַי] and their flocks [וְצֹאנָם] and their herds [וּבְקָרָם] and all that is theirs [וְכָל-אֲשֶׁר לָהֶם] have come [בָּאוּ] from the land of Canaan [מֵאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] — and behold [וְהִנָּם] they are in the land of Goshen [בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן].

**47:2** And from the end of his brothers [וּמִקְצֵה אֶחָיו] he took five men [לָקַח חֲמִשָּׁה אֲנָשִׁים] and set them before Pharaoh [וַיַּצִּגֵם לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה].

**47:3** And Pharaoh said to his brothers [וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה אֶל-אֶחָיו]: What is your occupation [מַה-מַּעֲשֵׂיכֶם]? And they said to Pharaoh [וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֶל-פַּרְעֹה]: Shepherds of flocks are your servants [רֹעֵי צֹאן עֲבָדֶיךָ] — both we and our fathers [גַּם-אֲנַחְנוּ גַּם-אֲבוֹתֵינוּ].

**47:4** And they said to Pharaoh [וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֶל-פַּרְעֹה]: To sojourn in the land [לָגוּר בָּאָרֶץ] we have come [בָּאנוּ] — for there is no pasture [כִּי-אֵין מִרְעֶה] for the flocks that are your servants' [לַצֹּאן אֲשֶׁר לַעֲבָדֶיךָ] — for the famine is severe [כִּי-כָבֵד הָרָעָב] in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] — and now [וְעַתָּה] let your servants dwell [יֵשְׁבוּ-נָא עֲבָדֶיךָ] in the land of Goshen [בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן].

**47:5** And Pharaoh spoke [וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה] to Joseph [אֶל-יוֹסֵף] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Your father [אָבִיךָ] and your brothers [וְאַחֶיךָ] have come to you [בָּאוּ אֵלֶיךָ].

**47:6** The land of Egypt [אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם] is before you [לְפָנֶיךָ] — in the best of the land [בְּמֵיטַב הָאָרֶץ] settle your father and your brothers [הוֹשֵׁב אֶת-אָבִיךָ וְאֶת-אַחֶיךָ] — let them dwell in the land of Goshen [יֵשְׁבוּ בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן] — and if you know [וְאִם-יָדַעְתָּ] that there are capable men among them [וְיֵשׁ-בָּם אַנְשֵׁי-חַיִל] then set them [וְשַׂמְתָּם] as chiefs of livestock [שָׂרֵי מִקְנֶה] over all that is mine [עַל-אֲשֶׁר-לִי].

---

*Jacob Blesses Pharaoh — The Years of Sojourning (vv. 7–10)*

**47:7** And Joseph brought [וַיָּבֵא יוֹסֵף] Jacob his father [אֶת-יַעֲקֹב אָבִיו] and set him before Pharaoh [וַיַּעֲמִדֵהוּ לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה] — and Jacob blessed Pharaoh [וַיְבָרֶךְ יַעֲקֹב אֶת-פַּרְעֹה].

**47:8** And Pharaoh said to Jacob [וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה אֶל-יַעֲקֹב]: How many are the days of the years of your life [כַּמָּה יְמֵי שְׁנֵי חַיֶּיךָ]?

**47:9** And Jacob said to Pharaoh [וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֶל-פַּרְעֹה]: The days of the years of my sojournings [יְמֵי שְׁנֵי מְגוּרַי] are one hundred and thirty years [שְׁלֹשִׁים וּמְאַת שָׁנָה] — few [מְעַט] and evil [וְרָעִים] have been the days of the years of my life [הָיוּ יְמֵי שְׁנֵי חַיַּי] — and they have not reached [וְלֹא הִשִּׂיגוּ] the days of the years of the life of my fathers [אֶת-יְמֵי שְׁנֵי חַיֵּי אֲבֹתַי] in the days of their sojournings [בִּימֵי מְגוּרֵיהֶם].

**47:10** And Jacob blessed Pharaoh [וַיְבָרֶךְ יַעֲקֹב אֶת-פַּרְעֹה] and went out from before Pharaoh [וַיֵּצֵא מִלִּפְנֵי פַרְעֹה].

---

*Joseph Settles His Family in the Land of Rameses (vv. 11–12)*

**47:11** And Joseph settled his father [וַיּוֹשֵׁב יוֹסֵף אֶת-אָבִיו] and his brothers [וְאֶת-אֶחָיו] and gave them a possession [וַיִּתֵּן לָהֶם אֲחֻזָּה] in the land of Egypt [בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם] — in the best of the land [בְּמֵיטַב הָאָרֶץ] — in the land of Rameses [בְּאֶרֶץ רַעְמְסֵס] — as Pharaoh had commanded [כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה פַרְעֹה].

**47:12** And Joseph supplied [וַיְכַלְכֵּל יוֹסֵף] his father [אֶת-אָבִיו] and his brothers [וְאֶת-אֶחָיו] and all his father's household [וְאֵת כָּל-בֵּית אָבִיו] with bread [לֶחֶם] according to the mouth of the little ones [לְפִי הַטָּף].

---

*Joseph's Famine Administration — Land, Livestock, and People for Pharaoh (vv. 13–26)*

**47:13** And bread was not [וְלֶחֶם אֵין] in all the land [בְּכָל-הָאָרֶץ] — for the famine was very severe [כִּי-כָבֵד הָרָעָב מְאֹד] — and the land of Egypt [וַתֵּלַהּ אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם] and the land of Canaan [וְאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] languished [מִפְּנֵי הָרָעָב] because of the famine.

**47:14** And Joseph gathered [וַיְלַקֵּט יוֹסֵף] all the silver [אֶת-כָּל-הַכֶּסֶף] that was found [הַנִּמְצָא] in the land of Egypt [בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם] and in the land of Canaan [וּבְאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] for the grain that they were buying [בַּשֶּׁבֶר אֲשֶׁר-הֵם שֹׁבְרִים] — and Joseph brought [וַיָּבֵא יוֹסֵף] the silver [אֶת-הַכֶּסֶף] to the house of Pharaoh [בֵּיתָה פַרְעֹה].

**47:15** And the silver was exhausted [וַיִּתֹּם הַכֶּסֶף] from the land of Egypt [מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם] and from the land of Canaan [וּמֵאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] — and all Egypt came [וַיָּבֹאוּ כָל-מִצְרַיִם] to Joseph [אֶל-יוֹסֵף] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Give us bread [הָבָה-לָּנוּ לֶחֶם] — for why should we die [וְלָמָּה נָמוּת] before you [נֶגְדֶּךָ] — for the silver is gone [כִּי אָפֵס כָּסֶף].

**47:16** And Joseph said [וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף]: Give your livestock [הָבוּ מִקְנֵיכֶם] — and I will give to you [וְאֶתְּנָה לָכֶם] for your livestock [בְּמִקְנֵיכֶם] if the silver is gone [אִם-אָפֵס כָּסֶף].

**47:17** And they brought [וַיָּבִיאוּ] their livestock [אֶת-מִקְנֵיהֶם] to Joseph [אֶל-יוֹסֵף] — and Joseph gave them bread [וַיִּתֵּן לָהֶם יוֹסֵף לֶחֶם] for the horses [בַּסּוּסִים] and for the livestock of the flock [וּבְמִקְנֵה הַצֹּאן] and for the livestock of the herd [וּבְמִקְנֵה הַבָּקָר] and for the donkeys [וּבַחֲמֹרִים] — and he supplied them with bread [וַיְנַהֲלֵם בַּלֶּחֶם] for all their livestock [בְּכָל-מִקְנֵהֶם] in that year [בַּשָּׁנָה הַהִוא].

**47:18** And that year ended [וַתִּתֹּם הַשָּׁנָה הַהִוא] — and they came to him [וַיָּבֹאוּ אֵלָיו] in the second year [בַּשָּׁנָה הַשֵּׁנִית] and said to him [וַיֹּאמְרוּ לוֹ]: We will not conceal from my lord [לֹא-נְכַחֵד מֵאֲדֹנִי] — for the silver is gone [כִּי אִם-תַּם הַכֶּסֶף] and the herds of livestock are for my lord [וּמִקְנֵה הַבְּהֵמָה אֶל-אֲדֹנִי] — nothing is left before my lord [לֹא נִשְׁאַר לִפְנֵי אֲדֹנִי] except our bodies and our soil [בִּלְתִּי אִם-גְּוִיָּתֵנוּ וְאַדְמָתֵנוּ].

**47:19** Why should we die before your eyes [לָמָּה נָמוּת לְעֵינֶיךָ] — both we and our soil [גַּם-אֲנַחְנוּ גַּם-אַדְמָתֵנוּ]? Buy us and our soil [קְנֵה אֹתָנוּ וְאֶת-אַדְמָתֵנוּ] for bread [בַּלֶּחֶם] — and we and our soil will become servants to Pharaoh [וְנִהְיֶה אֲנַחְנוּ וְאַדְמָתֵנוּ עֲבָדִים לְפַרְעֹה] — and give seed [וְתֵן-זֶרַע] that we may live and not die [וְנִחְיֶה וְלֹא נָמוּת] and the soil will not be desolate [וְהָאֲדָמָה לֹא תֵשַׁם].

**47:20** And Joseph bought [וַיִּקֶן יוֹסֵף] all the soil of Egypt [אֶת-כָּל-אַדְמַת מִצְרַיִם] for Pharaoh [לְפַרְעֹה] — for each Egyptian sold [כִּי-מָכְרוּ מִצְרַיִם אִישׁ] his field [שָׂדֵהוּ] — for the famine was severe upon them [כִּי-חָזַק עֲלֵהֶם הָרָעָב] — and the land became Pharaoh's [וַתְּהִי הָאָרֶץ לְפַרְעֹה].

**47:21** And as for the people [וְאֶת-הָעָם] — he moved them to the cities [הֶעֱבִיר אֹתוֹ לֶעָרִים] from one end of Egypt [מִקְצֵה גְבוּל-מִצְרַיִם] to its other end [וְעַד-קָצֵהוּ].

**47:22** Only the soil of the priests [רַק אַדְמַת הַכֹּהֲנִים] he did not buy [לֹא קָנָה] — for the priests had a fixed allotment from Pharaoh [כִּי חֹק לַכֹּהֲנִים מֵאֵת פַּרְעֹה] — and they ate their allotment [וְאָכְלוּ אֶת-חֻקָּם] that Pharaoh had given them [אֲשֶׁר נָתַן לָהֶם פַּרְעֹה] — therefore they did not sell their soil [עַל-כֵּן לֹא מָכְרוּ אֶת-אַדְמָתָם].

**47:23** And Joseph said to the people [וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל-הָעָם]: Behold [הֵן] — I have bought you this day [קָנִיתִי אֶתְכֶם הַיּוֹם] and your soil [וְאֶת-אַדְמַתְכֶם] for Pharaoh [לְפַרְעֹה] — here is seed for you [הֵא-לָכֶם זֶרַע] and you shall sow the soil [וּזְרַעְתֶּם אֶת-הָאֲדָמָה].

**47:24** And it shall be at the ingatherings [וְהָיָה בַּתְּבוּאֹת] that you shall give a fifth [וּנְתַתֶּם חֲמִישִׁית] to Pharaoh [לְפַרְעֹה] — and four parts will be yours [וְאַרְבַּע הַיָּדֹת יִהְיֶה לָכֶם] for seed of the field [לָזֶרַע הַשָּׂדֶה] and for your food [וְלָאָכְלְכֶם] and for those in your households [וְלַאֲשֶׁר בְּבָתֵּיכֶם] and for food for your little ones [וְלֶאֱכֹל לְטַפֶּכֶם].

**47:25** And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: You have saved our lives [הֶחֱיִתָנוּ] — may we find favor in the eyes of my lord [נִמְצָא-חֵן בְּעֵינֵי אֲדֹנִי] — and we will be servants to Pharaoh [וְהָיִינוּ עֲבָדִים לְפַרְעֹה].

**47:26** And Joseph made it a statute [וַיָּשֶׂם אֹתָהּ יוֹסֵף לְחֹק] to this day [עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה] over the soil of Egypt [עַל-אַדְמַת מִצְרַיִם] — for Pharaoh the fifth [לְפַרְעֹה לַחֹמֶשׁ] — only the soil of the priests alone [רַק אַדְמַת הַכֹּהֲנִים לְבַדָּם] — it did not become Pharaoh's [לֹא הָיְתָה לְפַרְעֹה].

---

*Israel Fruitful in Goshen — Jacob's Final Request (vv. 27–31)*

**47:27** And Israel dwelt [וַיֵּשֶׁב יִשְׂרָאֵל] in the land of Egypt [בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם] — in the land of Goshen [בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן] — and they took possession in it [וַיֵּאָחֲזוּ בָהּ] and were fruitful [וַיִּפְרוּ] and multiplied greatly [וַיִּרְבּוּ מְאֹד].

**47:28** And Jacob lived [וַיְחִי יַעֲקֹב] in the land of Egypt [בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם] seventeen years [שְׁבַע עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה] — and the days of Jacob [וַיִּהְיוּ יְמֵי-יַעֲקֹב] — the years of his life [שְׁנֵי חַיָּיו] — were one hundred and forty-seven years [שֶׁבַע וְאַרְבָּעִים וּמְאַת שָׁנָה].

**47:29** And the days of Israel drew near to die [וַיִּקְרְבוּ יְמֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל לָמוּת] — and he called [וַיִּקְרָא] to his son Joseph [לִבְנוֹ לְיוֹסֵף] and said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ]: If now I have found favor in your eyes [אִם-נָא מָצָאתִי חֵן בְּעֵינֶיךָ] — put your hand under my thigh [שִׂים-נָא יָדְךָ תַּחַת יְרֵכִי] — and deal with me in covenant-faithfulness and truth [וְעָשִׂיתָ עִמָּדִי חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת] — please do not bury me in Egypt [אַל-נָא תִקְבְּרֵנִי בְמִצְרָיִם].

**47:30** But I will lie down with my fathers [וְשָׁכַבְתִּי עִם-אֲבֹתַי] — and you shall carry me out of Egypt [וּנְשָׂאתַנִי מִמִּצְרַיִם] and bury me in their burial place [וּקְבַרְתַּנִי בִּקְבֻרָתָם] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I will do as you have spoken [אָנֹכִי אֶעֱשֶׂה כִדְבָרֶךָ].

**47:31** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Swear to me [הִשָּׁבְעָה לִי] — and he swore to him [וַיִּשָּׁבַע לוֹ] — and Israel bowed down [וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ יִשְׂרָאֵל] at the head of the bed [עַל-רֹאשׁ הַמִּטָּה].

---

## Philological Notes

**1. miqtseh echav (47:2) — "from the end of his brothers" [מִקְצֵה אֶחָיו]**
The phrase miqtseh ("from the end/extremity of") is interpretively debated. It may mean Joseph selected five brothers from the outer edge of the group as they stood before him (i.e., the least imposing), or it may be an idiomatic expression for "some of" his brothers. The root qatseh denotes boundary or extremity. Targum Onkelos renders it "from some of his brothers," which is the most natural reading. The five unnamed brothers contrast with the named five daughters of Zelophehad elsewhere; the anonymity may be deliberate, focusing attention on Jacob's audience rather than the brothers'.

**2. megurai (47:9) — "my sojournings" [מְגוּרַי]**
The noun megur (pl. megurai, construct megurei) derives from gur ("to sojourn, dwell as an alien"). Jacob's use of the word is theologically precise: he does not say "the years of my life" but "the years of my sojournings" — identifying himself as a permanent alien even in the land of his fathers. The noun appears again in v. 9 as megurei avotai ("sojournings of my fathers"), completing the patriarchal self-understanding: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob all lived as gerim ("resident aliens") in Canaan. The word connects to the Abrahamic promise of land not yet possessed (Gen 17:8, "the land of your sojournings," erets megurekha). Jacob's summary of his life as "few and evil" (me'at vere'im) stands in stark contrast to Pharaoh's expectation of a dignified patriarchal account.

**3. me'at vere'im hayu (47:9) — "few and evil have been" [מְעַט וְרָעִים הָיוּ]**
The paired adjectives me'at ("few, scanty") and ra'im ("evil, bad, hard") applied to the "days of the years of my life" form one of the most arresting personal testimonies in the patriarchal narratives. Me'at contrasts with Abraham's 175 years and Isaac's 180; ra'im encompasses Jacob's biography of suffering — the loss of his mother's presence, the flight from Esau, the years of servitude under Laban, the death of Rachel, the rape of Dinah, the deception over Joseph's coat. The verb hayu ("have been") is in the perfect tense, a completed summary. Jacob does not complain; he reports. The phrase is without literary parallel in Genesis and gives Jacob's audience with Pharaoh a gravity unlike any other scene in the Joseph narrative.

**4. vayekhalkeil (47:12) — "and he supplied / sustained" [וַיְכַלְכֵּל]**
The Pilpel form of the root kul ("to sustain, contain, maintain") conveys intensive or iterative action — ongoing, comprehensive provision. The same root appears in 1 Kings 4:7 (Solomon's supply officers "sustaining" the king's household) and in Ps 55:23 ("he will sustain you"). The provision lfi ha-taf ("according to the mouth of the little ones") is an idiomatic phrase meaning proportionate to each family's need, with taf ("little ones, dependents") serving as a synecdoche for the whole household. Joseph's care for his family mirrors the earlier motif of his saving "many lives" (Gen 50:20).

**5. va-ta'lahh (47:13) — "languished" [וַתֵּלַהּ]**
The verb lahah (in the Qal, va-talahh) means to be faint, exhausted, languishing — the same root used in Lam 2:11 for children "languishing" in the streets. It is a vivid term for social and economic collapse. The subject "the land" (ha-aretz) is personified as an exhausted body. The phrase applies equally to Egypt and Canaan, universalizing the famine's reach and setting up the global scope of Joseph's administration.

**6. ve'et ha'am he'evir oto le'arim (47:21) — "he moved them to the cities" [וַיַּעֲבֵר אֹתוֹ לֶעָרִים]**
The MT's he'evir ("caused to pass over, transferred") with the people as object is the standard reading: Joseph relocated the population to urban centers where grain was stored, a known Egyptian administrative practice during famine. The Samaritan Pentateuch reads instead he'evid ("made them servants"), which would read "he made them servants from city to city." The MT is preferable on both text-critical and contextual grounds — enslavement is addressed explicitly in the people's own offer (v. 19) and Joseph's response (v. 25) rather than imposed unilaterally here.

**7. chesed ve-emet (47:29) — "covenant-faithfulness and truth" [חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת]**
This hendiadys appears elsewhere at Gen 24:27 (Abraham's servant praising the LORD) and 24:49, and at Exod 34:6 (the divine character proclamation). In each occurrence, chesed denotes the faithful, loyal love that operates within a covenant or relational bond, while emet ("truth, reliability, faithfulness") functions as an intensifying complement. The hendiadys as a whole means "genuine, binding covenant-faithfulness." Jacob's request that Joseph act with chesed ve-emet toward him is a formal invocation of the deepest relational obligation — not mere sentiment but covenantal duty. The established project rendering "covenant-faithfulness and truth" is preserved.

**8. sim-na yadkha tachat yerekhi (47:29) — "put your hand under my thigh" [שִׂים-נָא יָדְךָ תַּחַת יְרֵכִי]**
This is the same oath gesture as Gen 24:2, where Abraham's servant places his hand under Abraham's thigh. The gesture is unique to Genesis and has no parallel in other ancient Near Eastern texts. The yerekh ("thigh/loin") in this context is the seat of procreative power and generational continuity — swearing by placing the hand beneath it invokes the patriarch's descendants as guarantors of the oath. The oath Jacob requires of Joseph thus engages not just Joseph personally but the entire line that will issue from Jacob. The request that he not be buried in Egypt is a legal and theological claim: Jacob belongs to the land of promise, not to Egypt.

**9. al-rosh ha-mittah (47:31) — "at the head of the bed" [עַל-רֹאשׁ הַמִּטָּה]**
This is the famous MT/LXX variant. MT reads mittah (מִטָּה, "bed"), while LXX reads "at the top of his staff" (ἐπὶ τὸ ἄκρον τῆς ῥάβδου αὐτοῦ), reflecting an underlying Hebrew matteh (מַטֶּה, "staff/tribe"). The two words are distinguished only by the vowel pointing (masoretic vocalization, absent from the consonantal text). The consonantal form מטה is identical for both words. Heb 11:21 follows the LXX: "Jacob, when dying, blessed each of the sons of Joseph and worshiped, leaning on the top of his staff." The MT reading — Jacob bowing at the head of his bed, a frail patriarch giving reverence from his sickbed — is contextually natural and is the translation adopted here. The LXX reading may reflect a tradition where Jacob was still mobile and used a shepherd's staff. This is one of the most discussed text-critical variants in the Greek-Hebrew comparison of the Pentateuch.

**10. va-yishtachu (47:31) — "and he bowed down" [וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ]**
The Hishtaphel form of the root shachah ("to bow down, prostrate oneself") is the standard term for reverential bowing in the Hebrew Bible, used for worship of God (Gen 22:5; Exod 4:31) and honor before human authorities (Gen 23:7; 33:3). Here Jacob bows down — the gesture is ambiguous: is it worship directed toward God in gratitude for Joseph's oath, or reverence toward Joseph? Most interpreters see it as an act of praise to God — Jacob, having secured the oath, responds with worship. The LXX's use of the same verb (προσεκύνησεν) preserves the ambiguity. The image of the aged patriarch, dying in his bed, bowing his head in worship is a powerful closing image for the chapter and anticipates the death-bed scenes of Gen 48–49.

**11. vayiqrevu yemei-yisrael lamut (47:29) — "the days of Israel drew near to die" [וַיִּקְרְבוּ יְמֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל לָמוּת]**
The idiom of "days drawing near" (qarevu yamim) to mark approaching death recurs in Deut 31:14 (Moses: "the days of your death have drawn near") and 1 Kings 2:1 (David). The use of the name Israel rather than Jacob at this point is significant: it is the covenant name, the name given at Peniel (Gen 32:28), that bears the weight of the death-bed request. The transition back and forth between "Jacob" and "Israel" in these final chapters (cf. 47:28 "Jacob lived," 47:29 "days of Israel drew near") reflects the dual identity — personal and covenantal — of the patriarch in his final years.

**12. vayifru vayirbu me'od (47:27) — "and were fruitful and multiplied greatly" [וַיִּפְרוּ וַיִּרְבּוּ מְאֹד]**
The phrase parah vа-ravah ("be fruitful and multiply") is the precise language of the creation blessing (Gen 1:28) and the Noahic re-creation (Gen 9:1, 7) and the Abrahamic promise (Gen 17:2, 6, 20; 28:3; 35:11). Its appearance here — in Egypt, of all places — is theologically pointed: Israel, in the land of bondage, fulfills the primordial creation mandate. This sets up the transition to Exodus, where the fruitfulness of Israel (Exod 1:7, using the same verbal pair plus additional terms) becomes the trigger for Pharaoh's oppression. The word me'od ("greatly, exceedingly") heightens the fulfillment beyond ordinary growth.

---

## LXX Comparison Table (MT vs. Codex Vaticanus)

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|-------|---------------------|-----------------|------|
| 47:1 | "Joseph came and told Pharaoh" | "Joseph came in and spoke to Pharaoh" (εἰσελθὼν δὲ Ιωσηφ ἀπήγγειλεν) | LXX adds "coming in" (εἰσελθών); MT lacks the entry notice |
| 47:2 | "from the end of his brothers he took five men" [מִקְצֵה אֶחָיו] | "from some of his brothers he took five men" (ἀπὸ δὲ τῶν ἀδελφῶν αὐτοῦ παρέλαβεν πέντε ἄνδρας) | LXX renders miqtseh as "from some of" (ἀπό); MT's "end" more literal |
| 47:5 | "Your father and your brothers have come to you" | "Your father and your brothers have come to you; behold the land of Egypt is before you" (ἰδοὺ ἡ γῆ Αἰγύπτου ἐναντίον σού ἐστιν) | LXX incorporates v. 6 material into v. 5; different verse structure |
| 47:9 | "few and evil have been the days of the years of my life [מְעַט וְרָעִים]" | "short and evil have been the days of the years of my life" (ὀλίγαι καὶ πονηραί) | LXX ὀλίγαι ("few, scanty") for me'at; πονηραί ("evil, hard") for ra'im — close agreement |
| 47:11 | "the land of Rameses [בְּאֶרֶץ רַעְמְסֵס]" | "the land of Rameses" (ἐν γῇ Ραμεσση) | Agreement on the place name; LXX transliterates |
| 47:12 | "according to the mouth of the little ones [לְפִי הַטָּף]" | "according to the body/person" (κατὰ σῶμα) | LXX renders taf as "body" (σῶμα) rather than "little ones"; different interpretation of the idiom |
| 47:21 | "he moved them to the cities [הֶעֱבִיר אֹתוֹ לֶעָרִים]" | "he enslaved them as slaves from one end to the other [κατεδουλώσατο αὐτούς]" | Major variant: MT he'evir ("transferred"); LXX katadoulosato ("enslaved") reflects reading he'evid (Sam. Pent.); LXX aligns with Samaritan Pentateuch against MT |
| 47:22 | "for the priests had a fixed allotment [חֹק לַכֹּהֲנִים]" | "for the priests had a gift from Pharaoh" (δόσις γὰρ ἐδόθη τοῖς ἱερεῦσιν) | LXX reads choq as "gift" (δόσις) rather than "statute/allotment"; interpretive paraphrase |
| 47:26 | "a statute to this day [לְחֹק עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה]" | "a statute until this day" (νόμιμον ἕως τῆς ἡμέρας ταύτης) | Agreement in substance; LXX νόμιμον ("legal ordinance") for choq |
| 47:27 | "and were fruitful and multiplied greatly [וַיִּפְרוּ וַיִּרְבּוּ מְאֹד]" | "and they increased and multiplied greatly" (ηὐξήθησαν καὶ ἐπληθύνθησαν σφόδρα) | LXX uses different Greek verbs (auxanō, plēthunō) but agrees in emphasis on great growth |
| 47:29 | "covenant-faithfulness and truth [חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת]" | "mercy and truth" (ἔλεος καὶ ἀλήθεια) | LXX renders chesed as ἔλεος ("mercy, compassion"); standard LXX translation for chesed |
| 47:31 | "and bowed down at the head of the bed [עַל-רֹאשׁ הַמִּטָּה]" | "and worshiped at the top of his staff" (προσεκύνησεν ἐπὶ τὸ ἄκρον τῆς ῥάβδου αὐτοῦ) | The famous variant: MT mittah ("bed") vs. LXX matteh ("staff"); identical consonants מטה, distinguished by Masoretic vocalization; Heb 11:21 follows LXX |

---

## Structural Note

Genesis 47 operates on three narrative registers simultaneously — the intimate (Jacob before Pharaoh; Jacob's dying request), the administrative (Joseph's famine policy), and the theological (Israel fruitful in Egypt) — and their interweaving is architecturally deliberate.

**Three Movements:**

*Movement 1 — Two Audiences with Pharaoh (vv. 1–12):* The chapter opens with a double presentation scene. First Joseph presents five of his brothers (v. 2), who answer Pharaoh's occupational question (v. 3) and request Goshen (v. 4). Then Joseph presents Jacob himself (v. 7). The structural contrast is sharp: the brothers speak as petitioners; Jacob speaks as a patriarch who blesses the king of Egypt (vv. 7, 10). The verb barakh ("bless") appears twice, framing Jacob's audience like brackets. That an aged foreign shepherd-nomad blesses the most powerful ruler in the ancient world without any recorded hesitation is one of the most understated reversals in the patriarchal narratives — the bearer of the Abrahamic promise stands in dignity before Pharaoh. Jacob's self-description in v. 9 ("few and evil have been the days of my sojournings") is the only extended personal testimony of any patriarch in Genesis — and it is spoken to Pharaoh, not to God or family.

The sub-unit closes in vv. 11–12 with Joseph's administrative provision for his family: the land of Rameses (= Goshen by another name, reflecting the Egyptian term for the eastern delta region) and daily bread "according to the little ones." The verb vayekhalkeil ("sustained, provisioned") closes the bracket opened by Joseph's initial report of his family's arrival.

*Movement 2 — Joseph's Famine Administration (vv. 13–26):* This extended passage is a self-contained administrative narrative describing a three-stage process of economic consolidation: (1) silver for grain until silver is exhausted (vv. 13–15); (2) livestock for grain until livestock is exhausted (vv. 16–17); (3) land and persons for grain, constituting a permanent land-tenure transformation (vv. 18–26). The structure follows the logic of scarcity and desperation. The Egyptians' own proposal (v. 19) — "buy us and our soil" — is the hinge: they initiate their own enslavement, which Joseph formalizes and institutionalizes.

Two exemptions structure the narrative: the priests of Egypt are explicitly exempted (v. 22), and their exemption is explained by their state stipend. The fifth (chomesh, 20%) tax instituted in v. 24 becomes a "statute to this day" (v. 26) — an etiological note explaining a historical Egyptian institution through the Joseph story. The exemption of the priestly class from the land transfer (vv. 22, 26) and the resettlement of the population to cities (v. 21) are both consistent with what is known of Egyptian administrative practices during famine periods. Joseph appears here as a supremely competent administrator whose actions permanently transform the agrarian structure of Egypt.

The theological valence of this section is deliberately muted — the narrator neither praises nor condemns Joseph's policy. It functions as background contrast to the section that follows: while Egypt loses its land and freedom to famine, Israel takes possession (va-ye'achazu, v. 27) and flourishes.

*Movement 3 — Jacob's Life and Death-Bed Request (vv. 27–31):* The pivot verse is v. 27, which brackets the administrative narrative with a theological countertheme: "Israel... took possession... and were fruitful and multiplied greatly." The creation-mandate language (parah, ravah) signals that God's purposes are being fulfilled in the very place of Egypt's institutional collapse. Then the narrative zooms in from the communal to the personal: Jacob has lived 17 years in Egypt (v. 28), reaching 147 years — a number whose components (3 × 7 × 7) suggest symbolic fullness.

The death-bed scene (vv. 29–31) is composed with careful economy. The oath gesture (hand under the thigh, v. 29) invokes the full weight of covenantal obligation — the same gesture as Gen 24:2. The request itself (not to be buried in Egypt) is both a personal wish and a theological claim: the patriarchal body belongs to the land of promise. "Lie down with my fathers" (v. 30) uses the standard Hebrew idiom for death-in-continuity-with-ancestors; "carry me out of Egypt" (nasa'tani) foreshadows the Exodus itself — Jacob's bones as the prototype of Israel's corporate departure (Exod 13:19; Josh 24:32).

The chapter closes with the image of the old man bowing at the head of his bed — an act of worship that answers Joseph's promise with praise. The MT's mittah ("bed") places Jacob as a bedridden patriarch; the LXX's "staff" (matteh) preserves a more mobile image. Either way, the closing posture — bowing, dependent, grateful — is the appropriate response of a man who has lived "few and evil days" and yet received the faithfulness he asked for.

**Theological Unity:** Genesis 47 holds Egypt's institutional diminishment and Israel's covenant flourishing in deliberate juxtaposition. The land of Egypt loses its soil to Pharaoh; the land of Goshen becomes Israel's possession. Egypt loses its freedom; Israel multiplies greatly. Joseph administers both outcomes simultaneously — serving Pharaoh faithfully while protecting his father's household. The chapter thus completes the Joseph narrative's central reversal: the one sold into slavery now distributes life to the nations and secures his family's survival. The death-bed scene at the close turns from the geopolitical to the personal, from the public administrator to the dying patriarch — and the request Jacob makes of Joseph (chesed ve-emet, "covenant-faithfulness and truth") is the same virtue that Joseph's entire career has been demonstrating toward Egypt and toward his brothers.
